segments.caption: The video opens with an exterior night shot of the Royal Festival Hall in London, illuminated with blue lights and featuring Persian text overlay identifying the event as a concert by Mohammad Reza Shajarian and his ensemble. The scene transitions to the interior of the concert hall, where a group of musicians is seated on a wooden stage under warm, focused lighting. The camera focuses on a central musician with long hair and a beard, playing a stringed instrument resembling a tar or setar, dressed in a dark traditional tunic. He is accompanied by other musicians, including one playing a similar stringed instrument and another on a percussion instrument. The camera pans across the ensemble, showing four musicians seated in a row, each with their instruments and music stands. The perspective then shifts to a high-angle, wide shot of the entire group arranged in a semi-circle on stage, bathed in red and amber lighting, emphasizing the intimate and traditional atmosphere of the performance. The musicians are deeply engaged, with subtle hand movements and focused expressions, suggesting a live, expressive musical piece in progress. On-screen Persian text identifies the piece as 'Pish dar Amad Seh Gah' and credits Majid Drakhshani as the sarod and setar player.

segments.caption: The video opens with close-up shots of individual musicians, beginning with a bald man with glasses and a beard in a dark tunic, followed by a close-up of an older man with long, curly white hair and a beard, also in a dark tunic, playing a stringed instrument. The camera then pulls back to reveal a full ensemble of musicians seated on a warmly lit stage, arranged in a semi-circle. The central figure, identified as Mohammad Reza Shajarian, is seated and playing a stringed instrument with intense focus, while other musicians around him play various traditional Persian instruments including a tar, setar, and a keyboard-like instrument. The camera zooms in on Shajarian’s hands as he deftly plucks and slides along the strings of his instrument, highlighting the intricate fingerwork. The scene shifts to a close-up of another musician with dark curly hair and a beard, wearing a purple tunic, identified as Majid Drakhshani, playing a stringed instrument with deep concentration. The video concludes with a wide shot from the audience’s perspective, showing the entire ensemble on stage under warm, ambient lighting, with the audience in darkness, emphasizing the performance’s intimate and immersive atmosphere.

segments.caption: The video opens with a wide-angle shot from the audience perspective, showcasing a full ensemble of musicians on stage at the Royal Festival Hall, illuminated by warm, ambient lighting that highlights the wooden stage and backdrop. The musicians, dressed in traditional and semi-traditional attire, are arranged in a semi-circle, each with their respective instruments. The camera then transitions to close-ups, first focusing on a musician in a blue tunic with a striped sash, intently playing a stringed instrument with a plectrum, his fingers moving precisely along the strings. The next close-up reveals a detailed view of a santoor being played, with rapid hand movements plucking the strings, emphasizing the intricate technique. The camera then shifts to a musician with curly hair and a beard, wearing a patterned tunic, deeply concentrated as he plays a stringed instrument, possibly a setar or tar, with a microphone positioned nearby. The scene returns to the wide shot, showing the entire ensemble in synchronized performance, before cutting to a close-up of another musician in a purple tunic, identified as Majid Drakhshani, playing a sarod with intense focus, his hands deftly manipulating the strings and tuning pegs. The lighting remains warm and focused, creating an intimate yet grand atmosphere, capturing the emotional depth and technical mastery of the performance.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a musician in a purple tunic, seated and playing a small, ornate percussion instrument, possibly a kamancheh or a similar stringed instrument, with focused attention. Beside him, Majid Drakhshani, identifiable by his long dark curly hair and beard, plays a stringed instrument (likely a setar or tar) with intense concentration. The camera then shifts to a close-up of a different musician with long hair and a beard, wearing a dark tunic with a striped sash, playing a wind instrument (possibly a ney) into a microphone. A wide shot reveals the full ensemble on stage at Royal Festival Hall, arranged in a semi-circular formation under warm, golden stage lighting, with various traditional Persian instruments including stringed, percussion, and wind instruments. The camera then focuses on a close-up of hands rapidly plucking the strings of a santoor, a hammered dulcimer, with visible tuning pegs and colorful strings, belonging to the Santoor Player wearing a red and yellow patterned garment. Another close-up shows a musician in a red patterned tunic playing a stringed instrument with a bow, likely a kamancheh, with precise and expressive movements. An overhead shot captures the entire ensemble from above, highlighting the stage’s layout and the musicians’ positions. The segment concludes with a detailed close-up of the Santoor Player’s hands moving swiftly across the instrument’s strings, emphasizing the intricate technique and the vibrant colors of the instrument and the musician’s attire.

The central figure, Mohammad Reza Shajarian, is seated at the center, deeply focused on his stringed instrument, while Majid Drakhshani, to his side, plays with intense concentration. The camera pans across the ensemble, capturing the Santoor Player’s rapid hand movements on the hammered dulcimer, the Ney Player’s focused breath into the wind instrument, and the Kamancheh Player’s precise bowing. The stage is set in the Royal Festival Hall, with its distinctive wooden paneling and warm ambiance, and the audience perspective shows the full scale of the performance from the darkened auditorium. The musicians are dressed in traditional tunics—some in deep purples, others in reds and blues—adding to the visual richness. The performance is cohesive and immersive, with each musician contributing to a complex, layered sound, highlighted by the dynamic camera movements that shift from intimate close-ups to wide ensemble shots, capturing both individual expression and collective harmony.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a musician’s hands skillfully plucking the strings of a stringed instrument, likely a setar, with precise and rhythmic movements. The camera then pans up to reveal Mohammad Reza Shajarian, seated and deeply focused, his long hair and beard illuminated by warm stage lighting as he plays. He wears a dark traditional tunic with a subtle turquoise trim, and his expression conveys intense concentration. The scene shifts to a wider view, showing him seated at the center of the ensemble, with Majid Drakhshani visible behind him, playing a stringed instrument with a golden body and white fretboard, wearing a purple tunic. The stage is bathed in warm, golden light, with microphones positioned in front of each musician. The camera then cuts to a close-up of Shajarian’s face, capturing his serene yet intense expression as he continues to play. A brief shot shows another musician, the Ney Player, with long hair and a dark tunic adorned with a striped sash, playing a wind instrument into a microphone with focused attention. The camera returns to a medium shot of Shajarian and the Ney Player seated side by side, both immersed in the performance. The background reveals other ensemble members, including a percussionist and a Santoor Player whose hands are visible plucking strings rapidly. On-screen Persian text appears, identifying the piece as 'ساز و آواز سه گاه' with lyrics by Majid Drakhshani. The atmosphere is intimate and reverent, emphasizing the traditional Persian musical performance in a concert setting.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of two musicians seated side by side, both deeply focused on their performance. The musician on the left, wearing a patterned traditional tunic with red and brown geometric designs over a black shirt, is positioned in front of a large studio microphone, suggesting he is singing or about to sing. The musician on the right, with long, graying hair and a beard, wears a dark tunic with turquoise trim and is playing a stringed instrument, likely a setar, with precise finger movements. The camera slowly zooms in on the left musician’s face, capturing his intense expression and subtle mouth movements as he sings or breathes into the microphone. The lighting is warm and slightly reddish, highlighting the performers’ faces and creating a dramatic, intimate atmosphere. The scene then cuts to a wider shot revealing the full stage setup: the two musicians are seated on a wooden stage with music stands, and behind them, another musician in a purple tunic is visible, playing a stringed instrument—likely a tar or setar—adding depth to the ensemble. The background features a wooden panel design, typical of a concert hall, and the stage is lit with warm, golden tones. The camera returns to a close-up of the left musician, now showing more of his profile and the microphone’s proximity to his mouth, emphasizing the vocal performance. Throughout the segment, the musicians remain seated and focused, their expressions conveying deep concentration and emotional engagement with the music. The overall atmosphere is one of serene intensity, characteristic of a traditional Persian classical music performance.

The stage is bathed in rich red and golden lighting, creating an intimate and reverent atmosphere. The musicians, dressed in traditional dark or patterned tunics, are seated with their instruments—stringed and percussion—ready for performance. The camera then zooms in, focusing on the central figures: Mohammad Reza Shajarian, seated and playing his setar with intense concentration, and Majid Drakhshani behind him, also deeply engaged with his stringed instrument. The camera continues to narrow its focus, shifting to a close-up of the Singer with Patterned Tunic, who is singing into a large studio microphone with expressive facial movements, his dark wavy hair and red-and-brown geometric-patterned tunic clearly visible. His performance is emotive, with subtle shifts in expression as he sings, suggesting a deep connection to the music. The surrounding musicians, including the Ney Player and Kamancheh Player, remain visible in the background, contributing to the layered sound. The lighting remains warm and focused, highlighting the performers while keeping the audience in shadow, emphasizing the concert’s intimate and traditional aesthetic.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Singer with Patterned Tunic, his face illuminated by warm red stage lighting as he sings into a large studio microphone, his expression intense and focused. The camera then pans to reveal the Female Kamancheh Player, dressed in a blue tunic with orange trim and a colorful headscarf, adjusting her instrument with concentration. Next, the focus shifts to Mohammad Reza Shajarian, seated beside the singer, his long graying hair and beard visible as he plays his stringed instrument with deep focus. The camera alternates between close-ups of the two central musicians, capturing their subtle facial expressions and hand movements. A brief transition shows a wide-angle Audience Perspective, revealing the full ensemble arranged in a semi-circular formation on stage under golden lighting, with various traditional Persian instruments visible. The scene returns to a medium shot of the Singer with Patterned Tunic and Mohammad Reza Shajarian, now clearly showing Shajarian playing a setar with intricate fingerwork, while the singer continues to sing with emotional depth. In the background, Majid Drakhshani is visible, playing his stringed instrument with intense concentration. The stage is bathed in warm, ambient lighting, emphasizing the intimate and reverent atmosphere of the performance at Royal Festival Hall. The musicians remain deeply engaged, their movements synchronized and expressive, creating a cohesive and immersive musical experience.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of Mohammad Reza Shajarian, seated and deeply focused as he plays his setar, his fingers moving deftly along the strings. Beside him, the Singer with Patterned Tunic sits attentively, his gaze fixed forward, occasionally glancing at his music stand. In the background, Majid Drakhshani is visible, playing his stringed instrument with concentration. The camera then zooms in on Shajarian’s hands, highlighting the intricate fingerwork on the setar’s fretboard. The scene shifts to a wider view, revealing more of the Ensemble Musicians, including a percussionist and others in traditional attire, all seated in a semi-circle. The focus then moves to the Kamancheh Player, dressed in a red patterned tunic, bowing his instrument with precision. A close-up follows of the Singer with Patterned Tunic, his face illuminated by warm red stage lighting as he sings with emotional intensity into the microphone, his expression conveying deep engagement with the music. The atmosphere is intimate and reverent, with warm golden lighting enhancing the traditional aesthetic of the Royal Festival Hall stage.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a female kamancheh player, dressed in a vibrant red patterned tunic, intently bowing her stringed instrument with focused precision. Her movements are fluid and expressive, her left hand deftly adjusting the strings while her right hand draws the bow across the strings with controlled energy. The warm, golden stage lighting casts soft shadows, highlighting the intricate details of her attire and the polished wood of the kamancheh. The background features a geometrically patterned wooden panel, adding depth to the visual composition. The scene then transitions to a close-up of the Singer with Patterned Tunic, who is seated beside the female kamancheh player. He wears a red and brown geometric-patterned traditional tunic over a black shirt and sings into a large studio microphone with intense emotional expression, his face contorted with passion as he delivers the vocal performance. His dark, wavy hair is slightly tousled, and his eyes are closed at moments, indicating deep immersion in the music. The camera alternates between the two musicians, capturing their synchronized artistry and the emotional intensity of the performance. The lighting remains warm and focused, emphasizing the intimate and powerful atmosphere of the concert. The ensemble’s cohesion is evident through their shared focus and the seamless interplay between the instrumental and vocal elements.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a female musician in a vibrant red and orange patterned tunic, intensely focused as she plays a stringed instrument with a bow, likely a kamancheh. Her movements are precise and expressive, her fingers deftly adjusting the tuning pegs and bowing the strings. The camera then pulls back to reveal a wider stage view, showing a traditional ensemble performing under warm, golden stage lighting. The female kamancheh player is seated center stage, flanked by other musicians: to her left, a man in a dark green tunic with a striped sash plays a wind instrument (ney) into a microphone; to her right, a woman in a purple tunic and headscarf plays a small stringed instrument, possibly a setar, while seated before a music stand. Further right, a male musician in a red and brown geometric-patterned tunic sings into a large studio microphone with intense focus. The background features a wooden panel design with diagonal golden stripes, enhancing the concert’s warm ambiance. The camera alternates between close-ups of the female kamancheh player’s hands and face, emphasizing her concentration, and wider shots of the ensemble. The performance is deeply expressive, with each musician absorbed in their role, contributing to a rich, layered musical texture. The lighting remains consistently warm, casting a golden glow over the performers, and the stage setup suggests a formal concert setting, likely at a venue like the Royal Festival Hall. The musicians’ traditional attire, including patterned tunics and headscarves, adds to the cultural authenticity of the performance.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Singer with Patterned Tunic, his face illuminated by warm red stage lighting as he sings into a large studio microphone with intense focus, his dark wavy hair and patterned tunic clearly visible. The camera then shifts to a close-up of hands adjusting a stringed instrument, likely a setar, with a microphone positioned near the sound hole, suggesting tuning or preparation. The scene transitions to a wider shot revealing three musicians seated on stage: the Singer with Patterned Tunic on the left, a musician in a dark tunic with a striped sash (Ney Player) in the center, and a younger musician in a purple tunic (Majid Drakhshani) playing a stringed instrument with a double gourd body (likely a tar or setar) on the right. The background features warm, diagonally striped wooden paneling, enhancing the intimate concert atmosphere. The camera then focuses on Majid Drakhshani, who plays his instrument with deep concentration, his fingers moving precisely along the frets and strings, while the Ney Player remains seated beside him, occasionally bringing his instrument to his lips. The lighting remains consistently warm and golden, highlighting the musicians' expressions and the rich textures of their traditional attire and instruments. The ensemble appears deeply engaged in a collaborative performance, with each musician contributing to the intricate musical tapestry.
